notes.note: Ambitus, electoral bribery, had reached an unprecedented pitch in the consular canvass of 54 BC; Cicero returns to it as the dominant scandal of the season.

notes.anchor: the interest rate jumped from a third to two-thirds per cent
notes.note: The rate (reckoned monthly) doubled because so much cash was being borrowed for bribery, draining the money market; the figures are monthly interest, so roughly four to eight per cent a year.

notes.anchor: the syndicate of Memmius and the consuls with Domitius
notes.note: A coitio: the consular candidate C. Memmius struck a corrupt bargain with the sitting consuls of 54, L. Domitius Ahenobarbus and Ap. Claudius, to pool resources and rig the elections; M. Aemilius Scaurus, a rival candidate, was outbidding them.

notes.anchor: to be announced at the praerogativa
notes.note: The centuria praerogativa was the century that voted first in the comitia centuriata; its result, taken as an omen, often swayed the rest, so bribe-prices were quoted for securing it.

notes.anchor: depositing 500,000 sesterces apiece with Marcus Cato
notes.note: M. Porcius Cato (Cato the Younger), byword for incorruptibility, was made stakeholder for the tribunician candidates: each lodged a forfeit with him, to be paid to him if convicted of bribery, so that Cato alone policed the election.
